title: The part that performs wellness while falling apart inside
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The part that shows up, smiles, and says it's fine 

 I sat in a yoga class last week, legs shaking in pigeon pose, and I thought about how my body was sc...
htmlContent:
The part that shows up, smiles, and says it's fine

I sat in a yoga class last week, legs shaking in pigeon pose, and I thought about how my body was screaming while my face looked peaceful. That's the part I want to talk about. The part that performs wellness while falling apart inside.

You know this part. You've probably been her. She's the one who posts the sunrise photo but hasn't slept in three days. She's the one who talks about boundaries at brunch but lets everyone walk all over her by dinner. She's the one who buys the $80 journal set and never writes a single word in it. I know her because I've been her. I am her, sometimes still.

Here's the thing about this part. She's not a fraud. She's not pretending. She's surviving.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, would call her a manager part. She's the one trying to keep everything under control. She's the one who learned that if she looks like she's handling it, maybe no one will notice the cracks. Maybe no one will see the panic attacks in the bathroom stall at work. Maybe no one will hear the 2 AM sobbing into a pillow.

She's been doing this job for a long time. For some of us, she's been doing it since childhood.

Think about it. When you were a kid, did anyone have space for your real feelings? Did anyone say, "It's okay to fall apart"? Or did you learn that you had to look fine, act fine, be fine, or else something bad would happen? For me, it was the second one. So I built a part that could perform wellness. She could smile through anything. She could say "I'm good" when I was drowning. She could show up to therapy and say all the right things while my insides were a war zone.

And she's not the only one. There's a whole system in there.

The wellness performer and the one who holds the real pain

Janina Fisher, who studies trauma and the fragmented self, talks about how trauma splits us into parts. It's not a disorder. It's a survival strategy. When something is too much to hold, the brain puts it somewhere else. Some part of you carries the terror. Some part of you carries the shame. And some part of you gets really good at looking like everything is fine.

That's the wellness performer. She's the one who reads all the self-help books. She's the one who knows all the right terms. She can tell you about polyvagal theory and window of tolerance and attachment styles. She's read Bessel van der Kolk and Gabor Mate and Pete Walker. She's done the breathwork. She's tried the cold plunges. She's got a meditation app on her phone with a 300-day streak.

And underneath her, there's another part. The one who is still in the childhood bedroom, hiding in the closet. The one who still believes it's her fault. The one who is terrified that if anyone sees the real her, they'll leave. That part doesn't get to speak. That part doesn't get to be seen. The wellness performer makes sure of it.

Because if the real pain surfaces, what then? Who would we be without the performance?

I'll tell you who. We'd be messy. We'd be angry. We'd be sad. We'd be human. And that's terrifying for parts that learned that being human was dangerous.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker writes about the inner critic and the fight/flight/freeze/fawn responses in a way that made me feel seen for the first time. He names the parts without pathologizing them. He says, "This is what happened to you, and this is how you adapted." That's not shame. That's understanding.

The exhaustion of keeping up the act

Here's what no one tells you about performing wellness. It's exhausting. It's a full-time job with no overtime pay. You're running a constant background process that monitors everything. Am I breathing right? Am I sitting right? Do I look calm? Do I sound okay? Is my face doing the right thing?

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, would say that this is your nervous system trying to stay in social engagement. It's the ventral vagal state, the one that lets us connect with others. But here's the catch. You can't fake your way into ventral vagal. You can't think your way into safety. Your nervous system knows. It knows when you're performing. And it keeps you in a low-grade freeze because the performance itself is a threat.

Wild, right? The very thing you're doing to feel safe is keeping you unsafe.

I see this all the time in people who come to me for coaching. They're doing all the things. They're meditating, journaling, going to therapy, doing yoga, eating clean, taking supplements. And they still feel like shit. They're still waking up at 3 AM with their heart pounding. They're still dissociating through conversations. They're still snapping at their partner for no reason.

And then they feel worse because they're doing all this work and it's not working. They think it's their fault. They think they're broken. They think they're not trying hard enough.

But here's the truth. The part that's doing all the work is not the part that needs healing. The part that's hiding underneath, the one who's terrified and ashamed and alone, that's the part that needs attention. That's the part that needs to be heard.

And the wellness performer? She needs to be thanked and asked to step aside. She's done her job. She kept you alive. But now, maybe, she can rest.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz's book walks you through the idea that every part of you, even the ones you hate, is trying to help. There are no bad parts. Just wounded ones trying to protect you.

What happens when the performer finally stops

I remember the first time I let the performer stop. I was in my therapist's office, and she asked me a simple question. "How are you really?" And I opened my mouth to say "I'm fine," but something else came out. A sob. A raw, ugly, guttural sob that I didn't even know was in there.

And then I felt the shame. The performer came rushing back. "Oh god, I'm sorry, I'm so sorry, I didn't mean to do that, I'm fine, really, I'm okay." My therapist just sat there. She didn't try to fix it. She didn't tell me it was okay. She just held space for the part that was crying and the part that was ashamed of crying.

That's the work. Not getting rid of the performer. Not shaming her for doing her job. But letting her know that she doesn't have to do it alone anymore. That there's someone else here now. An adult self who can handle things. A self who can sit with the pain without being destroyed by it.

Peter Levine talks about this in his work on somatic experiencing. The body holds the trauma, and the body needs to release it. You can't talk your way out of a nervous system response. You have to feel it. You have to let the shaking happen. You have to let the tears come. You have to let the anger move through you without hurting anyone.

And that's terrifying for the performer. Because she's spent her whole life making sure nothing feels too much. She's the one who numbs out with Netflix or food or work or scrolling. She's the one who keeps you busy so you don't have to feel the emptiness.

But the emptiness is not the enemy. It's the doorway.

Learning to be with what's real

So how do we start? How do we let the performer rest and let the real parts come forward?

Slowly. Gently. With a lot of compassion for the parts that are terrified of what might happen.

Start by noticing. Next time you're in a situation where you feel the performer taking over, just notice. Don't try to change it. Don't judge it. Just say, "Oh, there she is. She's trying to keep me safe." That's it. That's the first step.

Then, when you're alone, try to check in with the part underneath. The one who's holding the real feeling. You might not know what it is at first. It might just be a sensation in your body. A tightness in your chest. A knot in your stomach. A heaviness in your shoulders. Just sit with it. Don't try to fix it. Don't try to name it. Just be with it.

And if you can, say something to that part. "I see you. I know you're there. I'm not going anywhere."

This is the work that the wellness industry doesn't talk about. It's not about positive affirmations or vision boards or ing abundance. It's about sitting in the dark with the parts of you that you've been running from your whole life. It's about letting them speak. It's about letting them be heard.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach writes about how we can learn to accept ourselves exactly as we are, even the parts we've been taught to hate. It's not about fixing. It's about being with.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work helped me understand that what happened to me was real, and that my reactions were normal responses to abnormal circumstances.

I'm not going to tell you that the performer will go away. She might not. She's been doing this job for a long time, and she's not going to retire overnight. But she can learn to share the load. She can learn that there's a self here who can handle things. She can learn that she doesn't have to do it alone.

And you? You can learn that you're not broken. You're not a fraud. You're not doing it wrong. You're a person with a nervous system that learned to survive in an unsafe world. And now, slowly, you're learning to live.

Stay with me here. This is the hard part. The part where you have to feel the feelings you've been avoiding. The part where you have to let the performer cry. The part where you have to sit in the discomfort and not run away. It's not pretty. It's not Instagrammable. It's not going to get you a viral post.

But it's real. And real is what heals.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to understanding and healing from complex trauma
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems and parts work
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's approach to embracing ourselves with compassion
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - Judith Herman's foundational work on understanding trauma and its aftermath

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
